title: Starting a divorce in NYC Supreme Court
disclaimer: Informational guidance only — not legal advice.

scenarios.headline: Your situation — an active divorce case is already open

sections.heading: Do not start a second divorce case
paragraphs: When a matrimonial action between the same spouses is already pending in Supreme Court, you generally should not file a new Summons With Notice (UD-1) or Verified Complaint (UD-2) to open another divorce case.
Filing a duplicate case can be rejected or dismissed because the court already has a pending matter between the same parties.

sections.heading: What to do in the existing case instead
paragraphs: If you need property division, maintenance (spousal support), counsel fees, or other relief, those requests are usually made inside the case that is already open — for example through an Answer, Counterclaim, Notice of Motion, or other papers filed in that index number.

Use Get Documents for the forms tied to your current procedural step (for example Answer or service-related forms), not the initial commencement packet, unless you are truly starting a brand-new case.
bullets: Confirm the county and index number of the existing case.
Identify whether you are the plaintiff (person who filed first) or the defendant (spouse who was served).
Ask the clerk or consult the court's matrimonial part rules for the correct next paper in that case.

scenarios.headline: How a new divorce case usually starts in NYC Supreme Court

sections.heading: Overview
paragraphs: If you are self-represented and there is no pending matrimonial case between you and your spouse, the case typically begins in the Supreme Court of the county where you meet residency requirements (for example Queens County).
The commencement stage forms in your package explain how to start the case. Two common paths are below.

options.title: Option 1 — Summons With Notice (Form UD-1)
paragraphs: This is the simpler path many self-represented filers use when starting a matrimonial action.
UD-1 is typically a short form (often about 2–3 pages). After it is filed, you must serve your spouse within the time limits set by court rules.
bullets: Plaintiff (person starting the case) and Defendant (spouse) names
Grounds for divorce (many NYC filings use irretrievable breakdown for at least six months under DRL §170(7))
Relief requested: divorce, equitable distribution, maintenance, counsel fees, and any other court orders you seek

options.title: Option 2 — Summons (UD-1a) + Verified Complaint (UD-2)
paragraphs: This path is more detailed and is often used when the case may involve disputes over property, maintenance, or other issues.
UD-2 (Verified Complaint) is longer (often about 6–8 pages depending on the version) and requires verified statements under oath.
bullets: Full names and addresses of both parties
Information about minor children, if any
New York residency facts supporting Supreme Court jurisdiction
Prior cases between the parties, if any
Specific relief requested: divorce, property division, maintenance, counsel fees, and other orders

scenarios.headline: Commencement forms for your divorce matter

sections.heading: Current step
paragraphs: Your workflow is at the commencement stage. Use the linked form pages and Get Documents to download blank forms for this step only.
After filing, you must serve the papers on your spouse according to court rules and keep proof of service.
